The short course is three-fourths of all the theft is by employees delivery men, it's not shoplifters. It's the staff and it's the guys who deliver the stuff that are stealing everything. And they are stealing, by the way. In the grocery store industry, the thieves have four times more net margin. They take four times more out of the net, out of the business than does the business owner. And they have no investment. And so Jack was working really cheap when I got him. He immediately started to escalate price through the rough because the intrinsic value of what he was doing was so great, let alone the non-intrinsic value. But what really became evident to me was where the money was was in the rage and resentment of the owner about them getting four times as much net as they were. All of the logical, it only got you so far. But if you could really tap that, money went out the window. We sold himself in front of the room, and he wasn't all that good when we had the executive groups, so VP and above, but mostly he had owners because we sold the small chains. A single audio cassette tape for $500. And they knew they were getting a single audio cassette tape. And what was on the tape was the ten theft tracks so you could walk into your store and in ten minutes or less, find theft, know who was stealing, know how much they were stealing. And I contend we might have been able to sell it for $5,000. And what sold it was the pitch was like nothing because there was no pitch of, and you get this, and you get this, and you get this because you didn't get nothing. You got, and really we could have given it a three by five card, I tell you the truth. And by the way, I can still do it. I can walk in there, I can do it like this. But technology, cash register stuff has changed a little bit, but not much. He had this story, which I heard by accident, but I said that's it, of going to this, so he's doing a seminar for, and the guy owned a bunch of grocery stores. And so he has them over to his house afterwards, big gated community, beautiful house. And the owner gets a jacket drink and he takes them to the front of the house and they're looking out the picture window and he says, is there a house over there on the hill across from us on that big hill? So he says, yeah. So you see it's about twice the size of my eyes. Who do you think owns that? I don't know. The store owner says, a Pepsi guy who delivers to my stores. Now, as soon as he started telling that story, no price, no nothing, right? Because they're just pissed off now. They're just madder in a wet hand. And so the intrinsic value of the tape, irrelevant, the intrinsic value of the amount of theft stopped in dollars and cents even, irrelevant. This was all about the value of being able to catch that son of a bitch and get him out of my store. And so the same thing is true here. We really want to get to our business being based on the deeper meanings, not on the shallow stuff. Because deeper meanings change everything. Hi. I'm in the insurance business, property and casualty, and they're about to release a program for our small commercial identified as those businesses paying 25,000 or less called BRICS, which is a business resource information center and knowledge system. And the basis of this is we're trying to switch from ensuring to ensuring and helping your business grow. We have four areas, sales of marketing, operations and human resource, personal development and loss prevention. So I have a couple of things. One, do you think that's a good... You should get together with Kevin O'Connell, by the way, on your loss prevention stuff for free. Is it... I'd be curious in your opinion in terms of whether it's a good position in terms of the intangible values versus intrinsic.
